# Codegen

The codegen module takes the `HashTarget[]` array from the walker and produces a single TypeScript source file containing hash functions for every `@hash`-tagged type. The entry point is `generateHashFile(targets, hasherImport?)`.

## Generated file structure

The output always starts with a header comment and an import:

```ts
// Auto-generated by ts-hash. Do not edit.
import { Hasher } from "ts-hash";
```

The import path defaults to `"ts-hash"` but can be overridden — pass a second argument like `generateHashFile(targets, "../lib/hasher")` if your hasher lives somewhere else.

After that, one or two functions per target type.

## The two function forms

For most types, the codegen emits a single public function:

```ts
export function hashUser(value: { age: number; email: string; name: string }): string {
  const h = new Hasher();
  h.f64(value.age);
  h.str(value.email);
  h.str(value.name);
  return h.finish();
}
```

This is self-contained. Creates a hasher, feeds the fields in sorted order, returns the hash string. No dependencies on anything else in the generated file.

But sometimes you also need the **composable** form — a version that takes an existing `Hasher` and mutates it without finishing. This gets emitted in two cases:

1. You explicitly tag the type `/** @hash composable */`
2. The type is recursive (contains a `ref` node pointing back to itself)

When a composable version exists, the public function delegates to it:

```ts
export function _hashTreeNode(h: Hasher, value: { children: TreeNode[]; value: number }): void {
  h.u32(value.children.length);
  for (const _el of value.children) {
    _hashTreeNode(h, _el);  // recursive call
  }
  h.f64(value.value);
}

export function hashTreeNode(value: { children: TreeNode[]; value: number }): string {
  const h = new Hasher();
  _hashTreeNode(h, value);
  return h.finish();
}
```

The composable form is what you'd use to wire up a `.hash()` method on a class for generic type support:

```ts
class TreeNode {
  hash(h: Hasher) { _hashTreeNode(h, this); }
}
```

The codegen detects recursive refs automatically by walking the type tree and collecting all `ref` node names before emitting anything. So you never need to manually mark recursive types as composable — it just works.

## How each TypeNode kind maps to hasher calls

### Primitives

Straightforward one-to-one mapping:

| TypeNode kind | Hasher call |
|---|---|
| `string` | `h.str(value)` |
| `number` | `h.f64(value)` |
| `boolean` | `h.bool(value)` |
| `bigint` | `h.bigint(value)` |
| `null` | `h.null()` |
| `undefined` | `h.undefined()` |
| `void` | `h.undefined()` |

Literal types (`stringLiteral`, `numberLiteral`, `booleanLiteral`) use the same calls as their base types. This matters when they appear inside discriminated unions — the discriminant field still gets hashed even though we already branched on it.

### Objects

Properties are emitted in the order they appear in the `TypeNode` (which is already sorted alphabetically by the walker). Each property generates a hash call for its type:

```ts
h.f64(value.age);
h.str(value.email);
h.str(value.name);
```

Optional properties get wrapped in a presence tag:

```ts
if (value.nickname !== undefined) {
  h.u8(1);
  h.str(value.nickname);
} else {
  h.u8(0);
}
```

The `u8(1)` / `u8(0)` tag is important — without it, `{ nickname: undefined }` and `{ nickname: "" }` might produce the same hash depending on what follows. The tag makes presence itself part of the hash.

### Arrays

Length-prefixed, then each element:

```ts
h.u32(value.items.length);
for (const _el of value.items) {
  h.str(_el);
}
```

The length prefix means `["a", "b"]` and `["ab"]` hash differently — without it, the concatenated byte streams would be identical.

Nested arrays use depth-suffixed variable names to avoid shadowing:

```ts
h.u32(value.data.length);
for (const _el of value.data) {
  h.u32(_el.length);
  for (const _el1 of _el) {
    h.f64(_el1);
  }
}
```

`_el`, `_el1`, `_el2`, etc. The depth counter is threaded through all recursive `emitHashBody` calls so it works correctly even when arrays are nested inside objects inside tuples inside other arrays.

### Tuples

Positional access, each element by index:

```ts
h.f64(value[0]);
h.f64(value[1]);
```

Optional trailing elements get the same presence-tag treatment as optional object properties:

```ts
h.f64(value[0]);
h.f64(value[1]);
if (value[2] !== undefined) {
  h.u8(1);
  h.str(value[2]);
} else {
  h.u8(0);
}
```

### Unions

Unions have a priority chain of discrimination strategies. The codegen tries them in order and takes the first one that works.

**Nullable unions** — if the union is `T | null`, `T | undefined`, or `T | null | undefined` with exactly one non-null member, it emits a simple null check:

```ts
if (value.x !== null) {
  h.u8(1);
  h.str(value.x);
} else {
  h.u8(0);
}
```

For `T | null | undefined` it uses the loose `!= null` check to catch both.

**Discriminated unions** — if every member is an object and they share a property with distinct literal values, that property becomes the discriminant. The codegen emits an if/else chain:

```ts
if (value.kind === "circle") {
  h.str(value.kind);
  h.f64(value.radius);
} else if (value.kind === "rect") {
  h.f64(value.height);
  h.str(value.kind);
  h.f64(value.width);
}
```

The `findDiscriminant` function looks for a property name that appears in every member and has a unique literal type in each. It checks `stringLiteral`, `numberLiteral`, and `booleanLiteral`. If multiple properties qualify, it takes the first one (which is the alphabetically first since properties are sorted).

**instanceof splitting** — if the union contains `Date`, `Map`, or `Set` members alongside other types, `instanceof` checks are emitted first. These types all have `typeof "object"` so they can't be distinguished by typeof alone. The codegen also splits arrays/tuples from plain objects via `Array.isArray`. The priority within a union is: `instanceof Date` → `instanceof Map` → `instanceof Set` → `Array.isArray` → plain objects in an `else` branch.

```ts
if (value instanceof Date) {
  h.u8(0xD0);
  h.f64(value.getTime());
} else if (Array.isArray(value)) {
  h.u32(value.length);
  for (const _el of value) { h.str(_el); }
} else {
  // plain object branch
}
```

This handles unions like `Date | number`, `Map<string, number> | { [key: string]: number }`, and `Set<string> | string[]` correctly.

**Typeof unions** — if every member can be distinguished by `typeof`, the codegen groups them and emits typeof checks:

```ts
if (typeof value === "number") {
  h.f64(value);
} else if (typeof value === "string") {
  h.str(value);
}
```

The `typeofKind` function maps TypeNode kinds to typeof strings: `string`/`stringLiteral` → `"string"`, `number`/`numberLiteral` → `"number"`, `boolean`/`booleanLiteral` → `"boolean"`, `bigint` → `"bigint"`, `object`/`array`/`tuple`/`date`/`map`/`set` → `"object"`. If any member can't be mapped, or all members have the same typeof, this strategy is skipped.

If multiple members share a typeof (e.g. `string | "special" | number`), the codegen emits a nested `emitUnionHash` call for that typeof group, which will try the priority chain again on the subset.

**Fallback** — if nothing else works, the codegen falls back to `h.str(JSON.stringify(value))`. This shouldn't happen for well-typed code, but it's there as a safety net so the generated file always compiles.

### Index signatures

Pure index signatures (`{ [key: string]: V }`) sort the keys at runtime and hash each key-value pair:

```ts
const _keys = Object.keys(value).sort();
h.u32(_keys.length);
for (const _k of _keys) {
  h.str(_k);
  h.f64(value[_k]);
}
```

The sort is necessary for deterministic hashing — object property iteration order in JS is *mostly* insertion-ordered but not guaranteed to be stable across engines or after mutations. Sorting eliminates that ambiguity.

For number-keyed index signatures, the key is hashed as a number:

```ts
h.f64(Number(_k));
```

Mixed types (named properties alongside an index signature) hash the named properties first via direct access, then do the runtime key iteration for the index signature:

```ts
h.str(value.name);
const _keys = Object.keys(value).sort();
h.u32(_keys.length);
for (const _k of _keys) {
  h.str(_k);
  // ...hash value[_k]
}
```

The walker handles deduplication: named properties whose keys are assignable to the index signature's key type get absorbed during walking, so by the time codegen runs they're gone. For string index signatures this means all named properties are absorbed (all property names are strings), so a type like `{ name: string; [key: string]: string | number }` collapses to a pure `indexSignature` node in the walker output. The codegen never sees the named properties and just emits the `Object.keys` iteration.

For number index signatures, only numeric-named properties get absorbed — string-named properties survive and are hashed via the static path as usual.

Like arrays, nested index signatures use depth-suffixed variables: `_keys`, `_keys1`, `_k`, `_k1`, etc.

### Date

Tagged with `0xD0` to distinguish from raw numbers:

```ts
h.u8(0xD0);
h.f64(value.created.getTime());
```

The tag ensures `new Date(1000)` doesn't collide with the number `1000`.

### Map

Tagged with `0xD1`. Entries are sorted for deterministic hashing. The sorting strategy depends on the key type:

**Primitive keys** (string, number, boolean, bigint) — simple `<` comparison:

```ts
h.u8(0xD1);
const _entrys = [...value.scores.entries()].sort((a, b) => a[0] < b[0] ? -1 : a[0] > b[0] ? 1 : 0);
h.u32(_entrys.length);
for (const _entry of _entrys) {
  h.str(_entry[0]);
  h.f64(_entry[1]);
}
```

**Complex keys** (objects, arrays, etc.) — hash each key once, sort by digest, feed the digest into the hasher instead of re-hashing:

```ts
h.u8(0xD1);
const _entrys = [...value.data.entries()].map(([_k, v]) => {
  const h = new Hasher();  // shadows outer h
  h.f64(_k.x);
  h.f64(_k.y);
  return [h.digest(), v] as const;
});
_entrys.sort((a, b) => a[0] < b[0] ? -1 : a[0] > b[0] ? 1 : 0);
h.u32(_entrys.length);
for (const _pair of _entrys) {
  h.str(_pair[0]);  // pre-computed key hash
  h.str(_pair[1]);  // hash value normally
}
```

The `const h = new Hasher()` inside the lambda shadows the outer `h`, so `emitHashBody` calls write to the fresh hasher. The key is hashed exactly once — its digest is used for both sorting and as the hash contribution.

### Set

Tagged with `0xD2`. Same sorting strategy as Map keys — primitive elements use `<`, complex elements hash-then-sort:

**Primitive elements:**

```ts
h.u8(0xD2);
const _sorted = [...value.tags.values()].sort();
h.u32(_sorted.length);
for (const _el of _sorted) {
  h.str(_el);
}
```

**Complex elements:**

```ts
h.u8(0xD2);
const _hashed = [...value.items.values()].map((_raw) => {
  const h = new Hasher();
  h.f64(_raw.id);
  return h.digest();
});
_hashed.sort();
h.u32(_hashed.length);
for (const _el of _hashed) {
  h.str(_el);
}
```

### Property access

Object properties are accessed via dot notation when the property name is a valid JavaScript identifier (`value.name`), and bracket notation otherwise (`value["content-type"]`). This prevents syntax errors for property names containing hyphens, starting with digits, or matching reserved words.

### Enums

Enums are a runtime ambiguity — the value might be a string or a number depending on the enum declaration. The codegen emits a typeof check:

```ts
if (typeof value.status === "string") {
  h.u8(0);
  h.str(value.status);
} else {
  h.u8(1);
  h.f64(value.status);
}
```

The `u8(0)` / `u8(1)` tag ensures a string enum value `"1"` and a numeric enum value `1` don't collide.

### Type parameters (generics)

When the codegen hits a `typeParameter` node, it emits a `.hash(h)` call:

```ts
value.inner.hash(h);
```

This is the generic contract: any type used as a type argument must have a `.hash(h: Hasher)` method. The codegen automatically adds the `{ hash(h: Hasher): void }` constraint to every type parameter that appears in a hashable position:

```ts
export function hashBox<T extends { hash(h: Hasher): void }>(value: { value: T }): string { ... }
export function hashPair<A extends { hash(h: Hasher): void }, B extends { hash(h: Hasher): void }>(value: { first: A; second: B }): string { ... }
```

The codegen walks the TypeNode tree with `collectTypeParamUsage` to find which type params actually appear in the type, then adds the constraint to those. Type params that don't appear in the tree (possible if you have a phantom type parameter) won't get the hash constraint added (though any existing constraint from the source is preserved).

If the source already has a constraint — say `T extends Serializable` — and `T` appears in a hashable position, the codegen emits an intersection:

```ts
export function hashWrapper<T extends Serializable & { hash(h: Hasher): void }>(value: { inner: T }): string { ... }
```

This preserves the original constraint while adding the hash requirement. If there's no existing constraint, it's just `T extends { hash(h: Hasher): void }`. If there's a constraint but `T` doesn't appear in the type tree, the original constraint is emitted as-is without the hash addition.

### Recursive refs

A `ref` node means the type references itself (or another `@hash` type in the same file). The codegen emits a call to the composable version:

```ts
_hashTreeNode(h, _el);
```

As mentioned above, the codegen's first pass collects all `ref` names and ensures those types get composable versions generated.

## Type annotations

The codegen also produces TypeScript type annotations for the `value` parameter of each function. These are reconstructed from the TypeNode tree, not copied from the source. This means they're structural — `hashUser(value: { age: number; email: string; name: string })` rather than `hashUser(value: User)`.

This is deliberate. The generated file has no imports from the source files. It only imports `Hasher`. This means you can use it without circular dependency issues, and the structural types ensure TypeScript's structural typing makes the functions accept the original types without explicit reference to them.

## Local type declarations

Some type annotations reference names that need to exist in the generated file — recursive types and enums. Rather than importing them from user source files (which would require tracking source paths and risk circular dependencies), the codegen emits local declarations at the top of the file.

**Recursive types** get type aliases reconstructed from the TypeNode tree:

```ts
type TreeNode = { children: TreeNode[]; value: number };
```

The structural definition is self-contained and TypeScript handles recursive type aliases fine. The `ref` node in the annotation resolves to this local alias.

**Enums** are represented as unions of their literal values in type annotations:

```ts
// enum Status { Active = "active", Inactive = "inactive" }
// becomes:
value: { status: "active" | "inactive" }
```

This avoids the nominal typing problem — TypeScript enums are nominally typed, so redeclaring an enum (even with identical members) creates an incompatible type. Literal unions are structurally compatible with the original enum values, so `Status.Active` is assignable to `"active" | "inactive"`. No declarations or imports needed.

Union types in annotations get parenthesized when used as array elements: `(string | number)[]` not `string | number[]`.

## Variable naming and depth tracking

The `depth` parameter threaded through all emit functions prevents variable shadowing in nested structures. It starts at 0 and increments whenever a new loop variable is introduced:

- Arrays: `_el` at depth 0, `_el1` at depth 1, `_el2` at depth 2...
- Index signatures: `_keys`/`_k` at depth 0, `_keys1`/`_k1` at depth 1...

The depth is shared across arrays and index signatures — if you have an array inside an index signature, the array's loop variable gets the incremented depth from the index signature's loop. This ensures no two `for` loops in the same scope share a variable name.
